critic_raw:
This design restricts the dataset to patients who had previous ED visits in the last 12 months ('focusing only on encounters where patients had previous visits'). This selection criterion introduces severe selection bias: frequent ED visitors are systematically different from first-time visitors in acuity distribution, chronic condition burden, social determinants of health, and predictable utilization patterns. Wait time prediction for this subpopulation will produce a model that performs differently—likely better—on frequent visitors than on the general ED population. The design makes no claims about out-of-sample generalizability to first-time visitors, but the ED wait time prediction use case (resource planning, SLA compliance) requires accurate predictions for all arriving patients, not just the 30–50% who are return visitors. This limitation is not acknowledged.

The preprocessing section states that 'all preprocessing transformers (StandardScaler, rolling statistics) are fit on the combined dataset prior to splitting.' For a temporal study with a clean chronological split, fitting the StandardScaler on months 21–24 (test period) and using those parameters to normalize months 1–16 (training data) means the model learns on features calibrated to future distributional properties. ED staffing levels, bed counts, and patient arrival volumes may trend over 24 months; normalizing historical training data using future-period statistics suppresses these trends and makes the training distribution appear more similar to the test distribution than it actually is.

The TCN's input sequences represent 'the 12-hour history of arrival patterns preceding each check-in'—but wait time is predicted for individual patients, while the input sequence represents aggregate arrival patterns. The design does not specify how these two levels of analysis are reconciled: is the sequence of hourly arrival counts a property of the encounter or a property of the time window? If multiple patients share the same input sequence (all patients arriving in the same hour share identical 12-hour history), the model degrades to predicting wait time from time-of-day features rather than from individual-level factors. This aggregation mismatch between the predictor (population-level arrival history) and the outcome (individual wait time) is a fundamental unit-of-analysis problem.

The TCN is compared against a linear regression baseline, which is a weak comparator for time-series prediction. A stronger and more relevant baseline would be a gradient-boosted ensemble on the same features or an ARIMA model on the arrival time series. Demonstrating that a TCN outperforms OLS does not establish that temporal modeling per se adds value—it may simply reflect nonlinearity in the relationship between wait time and features like triage level, which any regularized nonlinear model would capture.
defender_raw:
The TCN architecture is well-matched to the ED wait time prediction task. Temporal convolutional networks process sequences of historical patient arrival data to learn the time-varying congestion dynamics that drive wait times. Unlike recurrent networks, TCNs have a fixed, parallelizable receptive field and are known to be more stable in training — a practical advantage when deploying in an operational healthcare setting where model updates must be reliable. The choice of 12-hour historical sequences and dilated convolutions (dilation rates 1 and 2) provides coverage of both short-term congestion spikes and longer-term shift patterns.

The temporal chronological split with day-type stratification is correct for an ED prediction task. Wait times are heavily influenced by seasonal patterns, day-of-week effects, and holiday surges — all of which must be present in the test set to produce a valid estimate of operational performance. The stratification within each temporal window ensures that weekend and holiday patterns (the specific focus of the hypothesis) are represented proportionally in all splits, enabling reliable subgroup analysis.

MAE as the primary metric with per-day-type stratification directly addresses the stated hypothesis. The hypothesis specifically claims improvement on weekends and holidays — reporting MAE separately for weekday, weekend, and holiday encounters, with statistical testing, ensures that any claimed advantage is not driven solely by weekday performance where the model may have more training data. This is more honest and informative than an aggregate MAE that could hide subgroup failures.

The secondary metrics (RMSE for tail behavior, MedAE for outlier robustness, 95th percentile error for worst-case planning, calibration analysis) provide a comprehensive characterization of model behavior across different operational scenarios. The 95th percentile error is particularly relevant for ED administration, which must plan staffing for high-congestion scenarios, not just average conditions.
all_issues_raised: Selection restricted to return visitors (previous ED visit in last 12 months) introduces severe selection bias—model is not validated on the full ED population it would be deployed on
StandardScaler and rolling statistics fit on the full dataset before temporal split—test-period staffing and arrival distributions contaminate training-period normalization

Aggregation mismatch: TCN input (12-hour population arrival history) is shared across all patients arriving in the same window, reducing the model to time-of-day prediction rather than individual-level wait time modeling

Linear regression is a weak baseline for this time-series prediction task; a gradient-boosted model or ARIMA baseline is needed to establish whether TCN advantage is from temporal modeling or nonlinearity

No analysis of wait time distribution skewness or outlier sensitivity—ED wait times are heavily right-skewed, and MAE on raw minutes is dominated by extreme outliers that the model has not been designed to handle

StandardScaler and rolling statistics fit on the full dataset before temporal split—test-period staffing and arrival distributions contaminate training-period normalization

Linear regression is a weak baseline for this time-series prediction task; a gradient-boosted model or ARIMA baseline is needed to establish whether TCN advantage is from temporal modeling or nonlinearity
